Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd tell myself I'd study, but then I'd just end up watching videos or scrolling through stuff online. When I did study, it was usually late at night when I finally felt guilty enough to open my notes. I spent maybe 17 hours a week on it, but a lot of that time wasn't super productive if I'm being real.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without looking at anything course-related, then suddenly realize something was due and cram for a bit. Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Regular study times just never happened for me - I kept meaning to set up a schedule but never followed through.
Q3: Usually it was because I got a notification about something being due, or I'd randomly remember I was enrolled in this course. Sometimes I'd log in just to watch the videos because that felt easier than actually reading or doing work. I logged in pretty often actually, but a lot of times I'd just browse around without really doing much.
Q4: Yeah, weirdly enough it did become kind of routine to just open the LMS when I was bored. I'd watch the videos while doing other stuff, like eating or whatever. It became this background activity for me. But I wouldn't say it was productive routine - more like a habit of checking in without really engaging deeply.
Q5: The videos were definitely the most useful for me since I could just watch them without having to put in a ton of effort. I watched over 200 minutes of video content because it felt less demanding than reading. When something was explained visually, it stuck better than when I had to read through dense text on my own.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums - I only posted once the whole semester. Reading through other people's posts felt like a waste of time, and I didn't really know what to contribute. Some of the supplementary readings I just skipped entirely because they seemed optional and I was already struggling to keep up with the basics.
Q7: If I could see how it directly connected to an assignment or exam, then it felt worth doing. Anything that seemed like busywork or extra I just couldn't bring myself to care about. I needed to see the immediate payoff, otherwise my brain would just check out and I'd end up doing something else.
Q8: Optional stuff might as well not exist for me. If it wasn't required, I wasn't doing it - simple as that. Required activities at least had some pressure attached, even if I still left them until the last minute. The optional stuff just felt like extra work that wouldn't count toward anything, so why bother?
Q9: Yeah, sometimes I'd download the PDFs and readings to my laptop and then study from those without being online. I also used some YouTube videos to understand concepts that weren't clicking from the course materials. My notes were handwritten sometimes, so none of that studying would show up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were times I'd be going over my handwritten notes or reviewing downloaded slides while lying in bed, totally disconnected from the LMS. I'd also think through problems in my head during random moments, like on the bus or whatever. That kind of mental review doesn't leave any digital trace but it's still studying, kind of.
Q11: Definitely. I'd open a video or document once, download it or screenshot the important parts, and then never access it through the LMS again. It was easier to have stuff saved locally so I didn't have to keep logging in. The LMS would only show that one access even though I might've looked at it multiple times offline.
Q12: Around midterms I just completely checked out. I was stressed about other stuff in my life and the course material started feeling overwhelming. My attendance dropped to basically nothing because I couldn't face going to class when I was so behind. The mindfulness exercises they suggested helped my mood a bit, but they didn't fix my grades.
Q13: Fatigue was a huge factor - I'd be too tired to actually focus even when I logged in. When I had competing stuff going on, the course always lost. Confusion about the material made me avoid it even more because it felt pointless to try when I didn't understand the basics. I'd log in, feel overwhelmed, and just watch videos passively instead.
Q14: All the time, honestly. I'd have the LMS open and be scrolling through materials but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I'd finish watching a video and realize I couldn't remember anything from it. Being physically present on the platform didn't mean I was actually learning anything.
Q15: I postponed studying for exams constantly, even knowing they were important. Something about the pressure made me want to avoid it more. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd say the same thing. It's like my brain just refused to cooperate even when I knew I was screwing myself over.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me do anything. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the motivation I'd been missing all week. Most of my assignment completions happened in the 24 hours before something was due. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have done anything at all.
Q17: When I got feedback on assignments, it sometimes motivated me briefly to try harder on the next one. But that motivation never lasted long. If the feedback was negative, I'd feel bad for a day and then kind of forget about it. It didn't fundamentally change how I approached the course.
Q18: The structure was pretty flexible, which sounds good but was actually bad for someone like me. I needed more forced accountability, more required check-ins or something. The freedom to work at my own pace just meant I worked at no pace. Having everything available at once made it easy to put everything off.
Q19: The way videos were organized made it easy to watch them, which is why I engaged with those the most. But the lack of structure around discussions and the overwhelming amount of reading made those harder to approach. If there had been smaller, more frequent required activities, I might have stayed more on track.
Q20: That's kind of me, actually. I logged in a lot and watched tons of videos, but my actual submitted work and exam performance didn't reflect that. I think it happens when you're going through the motions without really engaging. You're present but not productive - just existing in the space without absorbing anything.
Q21: I could see how someone who studies efficiently offline might not need to be on the LMS much. They might download everything once and then just work independently. That wasn't really me though - my low activity in certain areas matched my low understanding. But I get that logs don't capture everything.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention or understanding anything. They miss all the offline studying and the quality of engagement. Someone could have high activity numbers but be completely lost, like me with all those video minutes.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting real mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials - it's being present and invested. I wasn't really engaged this semester even though I was technically active. There's a big difference between the two.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how disconnected I felt from the whole experience, or how much I was struggling mentally to stay motivated. It wouldn't capture the guilt I felt every time I avoided studying, or the stress that made me check out. My emotional state and lack of real investment wouldn't show up in any log.
